A system is asked to generate an explanation from existing language resources. One tempting inference is: if it does not need new words, perhaps it can still assemble old words into something genuinely new. But that inference is blocked. The premises say that not needing new words does not establish that recombination yields new explanations rather than polished restatements of patterns already latent in training. So, in this world, verbal adequacy and explanatory novelty come apart.

Now place that system under evaluation with a benchmark modeled on a train-and-lightning thought experiment. The benchmark is supposed to test whether the system can recover an important explanatory structure. But the premises also say this benchmark risks target leakage, because Einstein’s solution is already known and structurally iconic. That means good performance cannot straightforwardly be read as independent rediscovery. The task format itself may carry enough traces of the expected answer that success reflects recall, attraction to a familiar template, or convergence on a heavily signposted cultural artifact.

These two points reinforce each other. If recombination of old words does not, by itself, prove explanatory novelty, and if the benchmark is contaminated by prior prominence of the target solution, then benchmark success provides especially weak evidence that something genuinely new has been explained. What looks like explanatory power may be only the smooth recomposition of already available representational material.

The next premise generalizes this into an epistemic problem. There is a “stack of mediation”: training data, representational encoding, benchmark design, and evaluative interpretation. That stack creates a loop in which apparent novelty could be an artifact of representational choices rather than discovery. So even when an output seems surprising or insightful, one must consider whether the surprise comes from the underlying world being newly understood or merely from a rearrangement of familiar symbols under a measurement scheme that mistakes stylistic or structural fluency for invention.

The final two premises introduce strings that appear semantically opaque: “Droughtiness therence eole cruddle reams” and “Nonnegativity bigbloom epidermically miscomplacence briming.” Since all premises are to be treated as true, these strings exist as part of the discourse. But nothing in the other premises assigns them a role, interpretation, or inferential link. Therefore they function as uninterpreted content within the same epistemic setting. Their presence sharpens the earlier point: representational form alone does not certify explanatory substance. A sentence can be grammatical-looking, novel-seeming, and still fail to ground clear discovery. If an evaluator were to treat such strings as profound merely because they are unfamiliar, that would be another case where apparent novelty is produced by representational choices rather than genuine explanation.

So the world implied by these premises is one in which language can be recombined impressively, benchmarks can overstate discovery because of leakage, and even strikingly novel-looking expressions may carry no justified explanatory gain. The central lesson is not that discovery is impossible, but that these conditions do not warrant concluding it has occurred.
